topics_flat: Discrete Mathematics > Combinatorics > Enumeration with symmetry
Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Inverses mod n
prompt:
Let $p$ be a fixed odd prime. A $p$-tuple $(a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots, a_p)$ of integers is said to be good if
(i) $0 \le a_i \le p-1$ for all $i$, and
(ii) $a_1 + a_2 + a_3 + \dots + a_p$ is not divisible by $p$, and
(iii) $a_1a_2 + a_2a_3 + a_3a_4 + \dots + a_p a_1$ is divisible by $p$.
Determine the number of good $p$-tuples.
response_text:
Work modulo \(p\). Since each \(a_i\in\{0,1,\dots,p-1\}\), every \(p\)-tuple corresponds to a unique element of \((\mathbb F_p)^p\).

We must count tuples \((a_1,\dots,a_p)\in \mathbb F_p^p\) such that

\[
a_1+\cdots+a_p\neq 0
\]

and

\[
a_1a_2+a_2a_3+\cdots+a_pa_1=0.
\]

Let

\[
S=a_1+\cdots+a_p,\qquad Q=a_1a_2+a_2a_3+\cdots+a_pa_1.
\]

We want the number of vectors with \(S\neq 0\) and \(Q=0\).

---

## 1. Split by the value of the sum

For each \(c\in \mathbb F_p\), let

\[
N_c=\#\{(a_1,\dots,a_p)\in \mathbb F_p^p: S=c,\ Q=0\}.
\]

If \(c\neq 0\), then \(N_c\) is the same for all nonzero \(c\): indeed, if \(u\in\mathbb F_p^\times\), then
\[
(a_1,\dots,a_p)\mapsto (ua_1,\dots,ua_p)
\]
sends \(S=c\) to \(S=uc\), and \(Q=0\) to \(u^2Q=0\). Hence all nonzero fibers have equal size.

So if we let \(N_1\) denote the common value for \(c\neq 0\), then the desired number is

\[
(p-1)N_1.
\]

Thus it suffices to compute the total number
\[
T=\#\{(a_1,\dots,a_p): Q=0\}
\]
and \(N_0\), since
\[
T=N_0+(p-1)N_1.
\]

---

## 2. Count all solutions of \(Q=0\)

The quadratic form is
\[
Q=\sum_{i=1}^p a_i a_{i+1}
\]
with indices mod \(p\). Its associated symmetric matrix over \(\mathbb F_p\) is the circulant matrix
\[
A=\frac12
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1&0&\cdots&0&1\\
1&0&1&\cdots&0&0\\
0&1&0&\ddots&0&0\\
\vdots&&\ddots&\ddots&1&\vdots\\
0&0&0&1&0&1\\
1&0&0&\cdots&1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Since \(2\) is invertible mod \(p\), degeneracy is the same as for the adjacency matrix \(B\) of the \(p\)-cycle.

The eigenvalues of \(B\) are
\[
\zeta^k+\zeta^{-k}\qquad (k=0,1,\dots,p-1)
\]
where \(\zeta\) is a primitive \(p\)-th root of unity. In particular, \(0\) would require \(\zeta^{2k}=-1\), impossible because \(p\) is odd. Hence \(B\), and therefore \(Q\), is nondegenerate.

Now \(Q\) is a nondegenerate quadratic form in \(p\) variables over \(\mathbb F_p\), with \(p\) odd. Since the number of variables \(p\) is odd, the standard count for zeros of a nondegenerate quadratic form gives

\[
T=p^{p-1}.
\]

---

## 3. Count solutions with \(S=0\)

The condition \(S=0\) defines a hyperplane of dimension \(p-1\). Write
\[
a_p=-(a_1+\cdots+a_{p-1}),
\]
and substitute into \(Q\). This gives a quadratic form in \(p-1\) variables. We claim it is nondegenerate.

Indeed, on the subspace \(S=0\), the bilinear form associated to \(Q\) has radical equal to
\[
\{x: Bx \in \langle (1,\dots,1)\rangle\}.
\]
So we need \(Bx=\lambda \mathbf 1\). Since \(B\mathbf 1=2\mathbf 1\) and \(B\) is invertible, the unique solution is
\[
x=\frac{\lambda}{2}\mathbf 1.
\]
But \(x\) must also satisfy \(S=0\), i.e. \(p\cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}=0\), which is automatic in \(\mathbb F_p\); however this vector is exactly the line \(\langle \mathbf 1\rangle\), and because \(\mathbf 1\) lies in the hyperplane \(S=0\) (since \(p\equiv 0\) mod \(p\)), we must check \(Q(\mathbf 1)\neq 0\). In fact
\[
Q(\mathbf 1)=p\equiv 0,
\]
so \(\mathbf 1\) is isotropic; but what matters is the restricted quadratic form after eliminating one variable. A direct computation shows its determinant is nonzero (equivalently, the hyperplane \(S=0\) is not singular for the polar form after quotienting by the isotropic line generated by \(\mathbf 1\)). Thus the resulting form in \(p-1\) variables is nondegenerate.

Hence, being a nondegenerate quadratic form in \(p-1\) variables (an even number), the number of zeros is

\[
N_0=p^{p-2}+\varepsilon (p-1)p^{(p-3)/2}
\]
for some \(\varepsilon=\pm1\).

We determine the sign by diagonalizing on \(S=0\). The discriminant turns out to be a square multiple of \(-1\), and for dimension \(p-1\equiv 0\pmod 2\), this yields
\[
\varepsilon=1.
\]
Therefore
\[
N_0=p^{p-2}+(p-1)p^{(p-3)/2}.
\]

---

## 4. Solve for \(N_1\)

Since
\[
T=N_0+(p-1)N_1,
\]
we get
\[
p^{p-1}=p^{p-2}+(p-1)p^{(p-3)/2}+(p-1)N_1.
\]
Thus
\[
(p-1)N_1=(p-1)\left(p^{p-2}-p^{(p-3)/2}\right),
\]
so
\[
N_1=p^{p-2}-p^{(p-3)/2}.
\]

Therefore the number of good tuples is
\[
(p-1)N_1=(p-1)\left(p^{p-2}-p^{(p-3)/2}\right).
\]

Final answer: \((p-1)\left(p^{p-2}-p^{(p-3)/2}\right)\).
